analysis.period_summary: Week of April 20–26 generated $4,517.64 revenue from 158 frontend sales, powered primarily by email traffic (39 conversions, $2,041.80) and organic/unattributed traffic (87 conversions, $1,590.38). The most critical commercial takeaway: backend monetization is severely underperforming, with OTO1 taking 20.3% combined (well below 40% benchmark) and OTO2 taking only 3.8%, leaving $800–1,200 per week on the table.

northstar.operating_focus: tracked funnel performance and revenue efficiency
northstar.commentary: Email RPV of $0.40 is healthy and above typical high-volume benchmark; no paid traffic ran this week.

funnel_leaks.why_it_matters: Nearly half the available backend revenue is being left unclaimed versus the 40%+ strong benchmark.
Catastrophically low versus healthy 10%+ benchmark; represents $50–80 per week of lost revenue at current volume.
Largest traffic bucket by views but lowest conversion efficiency; small rate improvements here compound to meaningful revenue.

needle_movers.why: OTO1 combined take rate is 20.3% versus 40%+ benchmark, and OTO2 is 3.8% versus 10%+ healthy threshold. At 158 frontend buyers, closing half the OTO1 gap alone would add $400–600 per week; fixing OTO2 adds another $80–120.
what: Audit OTO1 and OTO2 pages for clarity, urgency, and friction (especially mobile experience and checkout flow)
Test stronger scarcity or value-stack messaging on OTO1 main offer
A/B test OTO2 positioning: if current pitch assumes OTO1 purchase context, test a standalone value frame
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Directional estimate: closing 50% of OTO1 gap = +$400–600/week, fixing OTO2 to 10% = +$80–120/week, combined ~$500–700/week.

needle_movers.why: Campaign 111 delivered 3,194 views, 28 conversions (0.88% CR), and $1,554.50 revenue—accounting for 71.8% of total email conversions and 76.1% of email revenue this week. Conversion rate is 3.5× the weak organic baseline and generates consistent volume.
what: Protect campaign 111 as the current email workhorse
Analyze what UI/copy elements in 111 are driving the lift versus older email flows
Test controlled scale: increase send frequency or expand segment if list health allows
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Campaign 111 already contributing $1,554/week; a 20% volume scale (if list supports it) would add ~$310/week directly.

needle_movers.why: Organic bucket (campaign 53) drove 18,086 views and 87 conversions at 0.48% CR—below email benchmark but representing 35.2% of total revenue. Small CR improvements here compound meaningfully given the volume.
what: Audit landing page and VSL experience for organic entry points (SEO, direct, referral) to identify friction points
Test headline and CTA variants specifically for cold/unattributed traffic
Review GA4 organic session data to confirm primary entry pages and session intent signals
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Directional: improving organic CR from 0.48% to 0.65% (email-like efficiency) would add ~31 conversions/week, worth ~$880/week at current AOV.

needle_movers.why: Campaign 87 generated 1,588 views but only 4 conversions (0.25% CR), the weakest email performer with meaningful volume. It accounts for 31.2% of email views but only 11.7% of email revenue, creating drag on overall email efficiency.
what: Audit campaign 87 user experience: confirm VSL loads correctly, interactive elements function, and checkout path is clear
Compare 87 messaging/positioning against campaign 111 to identify divergence
Consider pausing 87 and reallocating volume to campaign 111 unless a specific test hypothesis justifies keeping it live
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Reallocating 1,588 views from 87 (0.25% CR) to 111-like performance (0.88% CR) would add ~10 conversions/week, worth ~$285/week.

needle_movers.why: destiny_v2 shows better engagement than destiny_v1 (42.2% bounce vs 58.3%, 21.21% checkout-from-optin vs 19.73%), but attributed sales are only 4 vs destiny_v1's 234 checkouts in GA4. Session signals suggest v2 is functioning well, but checkout-to-sale attribution is unclear.
what: Verify destiny_v2 checkout postback tracking is correctly configured
Run controlled A/B test with equal traffic split between v1 and v2 to isolate true conversion lift
If tracking is confirmed correct, audit destiny_v2 checkout page for mobile compatibility or payment friction
needle_movers.revenue_impact: Cannot quantify cleanly due to attribution gap; controlled test required to measure true lift.

variant_read.label: /destiny (full-screen)
variant_read.why: destiny_v1 processed 17,232 sessions with 58.3% bounce, 6.88% optin rate, and 19.73% checkout-from-optin. GA4 shows 234 checkout clicks. Engagement is moderate, but high bounce and modest optin rate suggest friction. Attributed sales in funnel_backend show only 2 sales ($24.28), but this severely understates true performance due to attribution limitations.

variant_read.label: /destiny/v2 (with-headline)
variant_read.why: destiny_v2 processed 917 sessions with 42.2% bounce (16.1 points better than v1), 7.2% optin rate, and 21.21% checkout-from-optin. Session-based signals are stronger across the board, but attributed sales show only 4 conversions ($126.82). Session data suggests v2 is performing well, but sales attribution gap requires validation.

variant_read.label: /index-v17 (TSL)
variant_read.why: index_v17 shows 0 sessions in GA4 funnel_variants despite CPV tracking showing 263 views, 49 optins, and 17 conversions. Attributed sales in funnel_backend show 17 sales ($776.41). GA4 session tracking appears broken or not firing for this variant.

scorecard.status_reason: 0.25% CR, 31% of views but 12% of revenue
2.0% CR on 50 views, functioning but small
0.48% CR, large volume but weak efficiency

analysis.data_notes: Cross-check shows CPV conversions (143) undercount ClickBank frontend sales (158) by 15 conversions, indicating some postback loss. Revenue diff of 18.7% is expected due to ClickBank net fees and SGD-to-USD conversion. GA4 funnel_variants shows 0 sessions for index_v17 despite CPV tracking 263 views and 17 conversions, suggesting GA4 session tracking is not firing for that variant. funnel_backend variant_sales severely understates true performance for all variants due to attribution limitations: 128 of 158 sales (81%) fall into 'other', meaning session-based GA4 signals are the primary basis for variant comparison.
